title: Androcalva viscidula
uses: Androcalva viscidula is a plant that has been traditionally used in Australian Aboriginal medicine. It has been reported to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, making it a potential treatment for various health conditions. The plant's roots and stems are often used in traditional medicine to treat fever, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. Additionally, Androcalva viscidula has been used to make a tea that is believed to have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory effects. However, further research is needed to fully understand its medicinal properties and potential uses.
taxonomy: Androcalva viscidula is classified within the family Cunoniaceae. This family of plants is commonly known as the trumpet trees or waterberries. The genus Androcalva is a part of this family and is native to Australia. It is a flowering plant that produces small, white or pinkish flowers. Androcalva viscidula is a specific species within this genus.

intro: Androcalva viscidula is a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae, commonly known as the sticky box or hairy box. The plant has been traditionally used in Australian Aboriginal medicine, and its extracts have been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, potentially offering relief from pain and wound healing. Bioactive constituents of Androcalva viscidula include triterpenoid saponins and flavonoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is often used to make herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and salves, which are used to treat a range of health issues, including wounds, skin irritations, and joint pain.
